prompt: A technician at the embassy must replace a failed component inside a staff laptop. Which of the following is a field-replaceable unit (FRU) that can typically be swapped by a technician in the field?

options.text: The CPU soldered to the motherboard
The GPU integrated into the SoC

rationale: SODIMM memory is a classic field-replaceable unit: it sits in an accessible slot, is keyed, and can be removed and reseated without specialized rework. Other common laptop FRUs include the battery, storage drive (M.2/2.5"), and Wi-Fi card.

optionNotes.B: A CPU soldered (BGA) to the board is not field-replaceable; replacing it requires board-level rework, not a field swap.
optionNotes.C: A GPU integrated into a system-on-chip cannot be removed independently in the field.
optionNotes.D: Internal layers of the display panel are not separately serviceable; the whole panel would be replaced.

prompt: A diplomat wants to quickly stop all wireless transmissions on a smartphone before a flight, disabling Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular radios at once. Which single setting accomplishes this?
rationale: Airplane mode disables all of the device's radio transmitters (cellular, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth) in a single action. Wi-Fi or Bluetooth can often be re-enabled individually afterward while cellular stays off.

optionNotes.B: Hotspot mode shares the device's cellular connection with other devices; it turns radios on, not off.
optionNotes.C: Do Not Disturb silences notifications and calls but leaves the radios fully active.
optionNotes.D: Power saving mode reduces performance and background activity but does not turn off the radios.

prompt: A laptop user wants to extend their single screen across two external monitors at a desk while keeping the laptop's own keyboard usable. Which accessory provides multiple video outputs, additional USB ports, and wired Ethernet through a single connection?
rationale: A docking station connects to the laptop (often over USB-C/Thunderbolt or a proprietary connector) and provides many expanded ports at once, including multiple video outputs, extra USB ports, and wired Ethernet, so the laptop becomes a desktop workstation.

optionNotes.B: A KVM switch shares one keyboard, video, and mouse set across multiple computers; it does not add ports to one laptop.
optionNotes.C: A USB flash drive provides storage only, not video or network expansion.
optionNotes.D: A Bluetooth dongle adds short-range wireless, not video outputs or Ethernet.

reference: Mobile Devices › docking station vs port replicator

prompt: On most laptops, a small icon (such as a sun or speaker symbol) is printed on the top-row keys. To activate that secondary function, the user typically holds which key while pressing the key?
rationale: The Fn key modifies the top-row keys to trigger hardware functions printed on them, such as adjusting brightness, volume, keyboard backlight, or toggling the wireless radio or external display.

optionNotes.B: Caps Lock only toggles capital letters; it has no effect on the Fn-layer functions.
optionNotes.C: The Windows/Command key opens system menus and shortcuts, not the printed hardware functions.
optionNotes.D: Esc cancels or backs out of actions; it does not enable secondary key functions.

reference: Mobile Devices › laptop function keys & features

prompt: An embassy specifies tablets whose displays must offer wide viewing angles and accurate, consistent color for reviewing documents from the side, while remaining LCD-based. Which LCD panel technology best meets this requirement?
rationale: IPS LCD panels are known for wide viewing angles and accurate, consistent color, which is why they are preferred where color fidelity and off-angle viewing matter. They are still LCD (liquid crystal) panels.

optionNotes.B: TN panels are fast and inexpensive but have narrow viewing angles and weaker color, so they shift badly when viewed from the side.
optionNotes.C: CCFL is a backlight type, not a panel technology, and does not determine viewing angle.
optionNotes.D: e-ink is a non-LCD reflective display used in e-readers; it is not an LCD panel option.

prompt: A smartphone's touchscreen still displays images correctly, but it no longer responds to finger taps anywhere on the screen. Which component has most likely failed?
rationale: The digitizer is the touch-sensing layer that converts finger contact into input coordinates. If the image is fine but touch input fails, the digitizer (or its connection) is the likely culprit, separate from the panel that produces the image.

optionNotes.B: The LCD/OLED panel produces the visible image; since the picture is correct, the panel itself is working.
optionNotes.C: An inverter powers the backlight of older CCFL LCDs and is unrelated to touch input; modern phones do not use one.
optionNotes.D: The proximity sensor only detects nearness (e.g., during calls); it does not handle general touch input.

prompt: Two staff members want to pair a wireless headset to a laptop. After enabling Bluetooth on both and making the headset discoverable, the laptop prompts to confirm a matching numeric code shown on both devices. What is the purpose of confirming this code during pairing?

options.text: To verify that both devices are pairing with each other and not an impostor
To assign the headset a static IP address
To switch the connection from Bluetooth to NFC
To set the headset's audio sample rate

rationale: Confirming a matching passkey/numeric code during Bluetooth pairing authenticates the two devices to each other, protecting against pairing with a rogue device (a man-in-the-middle) and establishing the trusted link/encryption keys.

optionNotes.B: Bluetooth pairing does not assign IP addresses; that is an IP-network function.
optionNotes.C: Confirming the code does not change the radio technology; the connection remains Bluetooth.
optionNotes.D: Audio sample rate is negotiated by the audio profile, not set by the pairing confirmation.

prompt: A traveling officer with no Wi-Fi available connects a laptop to a smartphone using a USB cable so the laptop can use the phone's cellular data connection. What is this configuration called?
rationale: Sharing a phone's cellular connection with another device over a USB cable is USB tethering. Sharing it wirelessly to multiple devices is a Wi-Fi hotspot; both let the phone act as a cellular gateway.

optionNotes.B: NFC is a very-short-range tap technology used for quick exchanges or payments, not for sustained internet sharing over a cable.
optionNotes.C: Bluetooth file transfer moves files between devices; it is not the mechanism for sharing the cellular link over USB.
optionNotes.D: Split tunneling is a VPN routing behavior, unrelated to how the laptop obtains internet from the phone.

prompt: An administrator configures a mobile mail client to retrieve messages so that they stay synchronized across the phone, tablet, and webmail (read/unread status and folders match everywhere). Which incoming-mail protocol is designed for this multi-device synchronization, and what is its default secure (encrypted) port?
rationale: IMAP keeps mail on the server and synchronizes folder and read/unread state across multiple clients, which is what is needed here. Its default implicit-TLS (secure) port is 993.

optionNotes.B: POP3 (secure port 995) typically downloads and removes mail to a single device, so it does not keep multiple devices in sync.
optionNotes.C: SMTP (port 587) is for sending outgoing mail, not retrieving it.
optionNotes.D: IMAP on 143 is the unencrypted/STARTTLS port; the secure implicit-TLS IMAP port is 993, which the question asks for.

reference: Mobile Devices › mobile email setup & ports (IMAP)

prompt: An embassy uses Microsoft Exchange for mail, calendar, and contacts. A mobile device needs to wirelessly synchronize all three and honor centrally pushed security policies (such as PIN enforcement). Which protocol is specifically designed for this?
rationale: Exchange ActiveSync synchronizes mail, calendar, and contacts to mobile devices over the air and lets the Exchange server enforce mobile security policies (e.g., require a passcode, enable remote wipe), which plain mail protocols cannot do.

optionNotes.B: POP3 only retrieves mail to one device; it does not sync calendar/contacts or enforce policy.
optionNotes.C: FTP transfers files and has nothing to do with mail, calendar, or device policy.
optionNotes.D: SNMP is for network device monitoring and management, not mobile mail synchronization.

prompt: A staff member's encrypted smartphone is lost during overseas travel and may contain sensitive data. The administrator wants to render all data on the device permanently inaccessible from the management console. Which mobile security capability provides this?
rationale: Remote wipe lets an administrator (typically via MDM) erase the device's data over the network, making the contents inaccessible. Combined with full-device encryption, it is the standard response to a lost or stolen mobile device.

optionNotes.B: Geofencing triggers actions based on location boundaries; it does not by itself erase the device.
optionNotes.C: A screen lock timeout helps deter casual access but does not destroy data and can be bypassed given time.
optionNotes.D: Locating the device may help recover it but does not remove the sensitive data from a device that may already be compromised.

prompt: An organization adopts a BYOD model and deploys an MDM solution. To protect embassy data on employee-owned phones without seizing personal photos and apps, which MDM approach best balances security and personal privacy?

options.text: Containerization that isolates corporate apps/data and allows a selective wipe of only the corporate container
Performing a full-device factory reset of the personal phone on enrollment
Requiring users to disable the device passcode so IT can access it freely
Storing all corporate mail unencrypted so it can be inspected easily

rationale: Containerization (a managed work profile) keeps corporate apps and data in an isolated, encrypted space separate from personal content, so IT can apply policy and perform a selective wipe of only the corporate container if the employee leaves or loses the device, preserving personal privacy.

optionNotes.B: A full factory reset destroys the owner's personal data and is inappropriate for a personally owned BYOD device.
optionNotes.C: Disabling the passcode weakens security drastically and is the opposite of protecting data.
optionNotes.D: Storing corporate mail unencrypted exposes sensitive data and violates basic data protection requirements.

prompt: A technician compares an OLED phone display to a traditional LED-backlit IPS LCD of the same size. Which statement accurately describes a fundamental advantage of the OLED panel?

options.text: Each pixel emits its own light, so black pixels are switched off, producing true blacks and very high contrast
It relies on a shared CCFL backlight, which gives it deeper blacks than LED backlights
It requires an inverter board to drive the per-pixel light, increasing reliability
It cannot display color and is therefore limited to monochrome e-reader use

rationale: OLED is an emissive technology: each pixel generates its own light and can be turned completely off, yielding true blacks, very high contrast ratios, and (typically) thinner panels and wide viewing angles, without any backlight.

optionNotes.B: OLED has no backlight at all (CCFL or otherwise); the emissive pixels are precisely why it beats backlit LCDs for blacks.
optionNotes.C: OLED needs no inverter; inverters were used to drive CCFL backlights in older LCDs.
optionNotes.D: OLED panels display full color and are widely used in premium phones and TVs, not just monochrome readers.

prompt: An officer reports that turn-by-turn location works on a phone even with cellular data turned off and no Wi-Fi connection, but address search and live traffic do not load. Which explanation is most technically accurate?

options.text: GPS positioning is receive-only from satellites and works offline, but map data, search, and traffic require an internet connection
GPS transmits the phone's location to satellites, which return the map, so it needs cellular to upload position
Location only works because Bluetooth is providing the satellite link
GPS requires an active NFC tap to obtain a position fix

rationale: GPS is a passive, receive-only system: the phone computes its own position from satellite signals without any data connection. However, downloading map tiles, performing address search, and fetching live traffic are network services that require internet access, which explains why positioning works but those features fail offline.

optionNotes.B: The phone does not transmit anything to GPS satellites; it only listens, so it does not upload its position to them.
optionNotes.C: Bluetooth is short-range and is not the satellite link; GPS uses its own dedicated radio receiver.
optionNotes.D: NFC is a centimeter-range tap technology and plays no role in obtaining a GPS fix.
